name: plugins
context: Add, configure, and author Vite plugins — official plugins, community registry, enforce ordering, conditional apply, and custom plugin hooks
info: Vite's plugin system extends the Rollup/Rolldown plugin interface with Vite-specific hooks for dev server middleware, HMR, and HTML transforms. Official plugins cover the major frameworks: @vitejs/plugin-react (uses Oxc Transformer for Fast Refresh), @vitejs/plugin-react-swc (uses SWC for even faster React transforms), @vitejs/plugin-vue (Vue 3 SFC compilation), @vitejs/plugin-vue-jsx (Vue 3 JSX/TSX support), @vitejs/plugin-legacy (generates legacy chunks for older browsers via @babel/preset-env and SystemJS), and @vitejs/plugin-rsc (experimental React Server Components support). The community plugin registry lives at registry.vite.dev/plugins and lists hundreds of maintained plugins for Markdown, PWA, icons, federation, and more. Plugins are added to the `plugins` array in [[vite/config]]. Each plugin is a factory function that returns an object with a `name` property and hook methods. Key hooks include `config` (modify resolved config), `configResolved` (read final config), `configureServer` (add dev server middleware), `transformIndexHtml` (inject tags into HTML), `resolveId` (custom module resolution), `load` (provide virtual module content), `transform` (transform module source), and `handleHotUpdate` (custom HMR handling). Plugin ordering is controlled via `enforce: 'pre'` or `enforce: 'post'`, and conditional application via `apply: 'serve'` or `apply: 'build'`. Rollup plugins that do not use Rollup-only hooks work directly in Vite; those that do should be listed in [[vite/config]] under `build.rollupOptions.plugins`. Virtual modules use the `virtual:` prefix convention and must resolve IDs prefixed with `\0` to exclude them from other plugins. See [[vite/dev-server]] for server-side hooks and [[vite/build]] for build-time plugin behavior.
triggers: use legacy browser support in vite
add a virtual module in vite

sections.title: Official plugins
sections.body: Vite maintains official plugins under the `@vitejs/` scope. @vitejs/plugin-react uses Oxc Transformer for JSX and React Fast Refresh. @vitejs/plugin-react-swc swaps Oxc for SWC — faster for large codebases. @vitejs/plugin-vue compiles Vue 3 Single File Components. @vitejs/plugin-vue-jsx adds Vue 3 JSX/TSX support. @vitejs/plugin-legacy generates legacy-compatible bundles using @babel/preset-env and SystemJS polyfills for browsers that lack native ESM. @vitejs/plugin-rsc provides experimental React Server Components support. Each plugin is a factory function that accepts options and returns a plugin object.

sections.title: Adding plugins to config
sections.body: Plugins are added to the `plugins` array in [[vite/config]]. Each entry is a function call (not the import itself — call the factory). The array is flattened, so you can nest arrays and falsy values are ignored, enabling conditional plugins: `plugins: [react(), isLegacy && legacy()]`. Plugins are resolved in array order, subject to `enforce` overrides.

sections.title: Plugin ordering and enforce
sections.body: Plugins run in order: alias -> enforce:'pre' -> Vite core -> normal plugins -> Vite build -> enforce:'post' -> Vite post-build. Set `enforce: 'pre'` to run a plugin before Vite core transforms (useful for raw source manipulation). Set `enforce: 'post'` to run after Vite's built-in plugins (useful for post-processing transformed code). Most plugins do not need `enforce` — only set it when order-dependent behavior requires it.

sections.title: Writing custom plugins
sections.body: A Vite plugin is a factory function returning an object with a `name` string and one or more hook methods. The `transform(code, id)` hook receives source code and module ID, returning `{ code, map }` or null to skip. The `resolveId(source, importer)` hook intercepts module resolution. The `load(id)` hook returns content for a resolved ID. The `configureServer(server)` hook adds Express-compatible middleware to the [[vite/dev-server]]. The `transformIndexHtml(html)` hook injects scripts, links, or meta tags into the HTML entry. Always return sourcemaps from `transform` for accurate debugging — return `map: null` if you cannot produce one.

sections.title: Virtual modules
sections.body: Virtual modules provide synthetic content at import time without corresponding files on disk. Convention: use `virtual:` prefix for the module ID (e.g., `virtual:app-config`). In `resolveId`, match the virtual ID and return it prefixed with `\0` — this tells Rollup/Vite the module is virtual and prevents other plugins from trying to resolve it. In `load`, match the `\0`-prefixed ID and return the generated source. Declare a TypeScript module declaration in a `.d.ts` file for type safety. Virtual modules are useful for build-time code generation, injecting configuration, and providing compile-time constants.

sections.title: Rollup plugin compatibility
sections.body: Vite supports most Rollup plugins directly in the `plugins` array, as long as they only use compatible hooks: `buildStart`, `resolveId`, `load`, `transform`, `buildEnd`, `generateBundle`, `closeBundle`. Plugins that rely on Rollup-specific hooks like `moduleParsed`, `renderChunk`, or `augmentChunkHash` should be placed in `build.rollupOptions.plugins` instead, where they only run during the production build. The compatibility list at vite-rollup-plugins.patak.dev tracks which Rollup plugins are verified to work in Vite.

sections.title: Community plugin registry
sections.body: The official community plugin registry at registry.vite.dev/plugins lists hundreds of maintained plugins. Popular categories include framework integrations (Svelte, Solid, Qwik), DX tools (auto-import, component auto-registration, TypeScript checker), asset handling (SVG-as-component, image optimization, Markdown), PWA support, module federation, and SSR helpers. When evaluating a community plugin, check its download count, last publish date, and compatibility with your Vite version.

related.note: A vite.config.ts must exist to register plugins in the plugins array
Vite must be installed in the project before adding plugins
